item.title: Woah
item.content:encoded: Really unique and absurd. With everyone trying to make cold-blooded keter class killers, this is definitely more interesting. I like how it's just not at all focused on the horror aspect—-it's entirely focused on just "ah gee, what going on with the register? pretty weird." people tend to forget that SCPs can be literally anything in theory, and I believe that they should always break expectation rather than abide to fantasy stereotypes. easy +1 for that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2738 is an unlicensed software

I think you're using "unlicensed" here improperly. I'm guessing what you mean is that the copyright for the software has not been registered with any governmental authority. If that is what you mean, there is no simple adjective that can be used to describe it. You could instead write something like

No POS software named ML has been registered with any copyright registration agency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My otherwise unused PhD in Economics was tickled by this one :) By the way, it seems like the assumption is nobody gets paid for this stuff when it transfers, but have appropriate accounts been checked? It's possible SCP-2738 could be creating currency transfers ex nihilo and just bypassing the cash register.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the idea and the execution - upvoted despite some mild misgivings about the necessity of the final interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally, I dig the hell out of anomalies that manifest in networks and patterns, but this just didn't do it for me. Maybe it just reminded me a bit too much of the article about the sapient corporation, maybe it just suffers because I just read SCP-2791, or maybe it's some other problem entirely. At any rate, I just didn't find much to be interested in here.

Technically quite competent, but -1 nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Welp. And fixed again!

I'm a graphic designer not a statistician and I had a chemical engineering student and a finance professional read this and no one caught it it's not my fault aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Without wanting to sound like a pedantic arse, it doesn't make sense for the graph to go "backwards" on itself just after March 2014. So if I'm trying to read off the number of instances around March 5th, the line crosses that point three times, so there are three different values it could be!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first draft of this was from so long ago that I legitimately don't remember, but that fits the theme perfectly so I'm just gonna say yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does 'ML' stand for Marxism-Leninism by any chance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crap, sorry. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Psst: still need to switch the larger version

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's because I made the graph in Photoshop rather than an actual graphing program (which I don't have, and I didn't want to have an obvious PowerPoint screenshot in an SCP article) and thus just wasn't thinking about it. I'll change the graph today.

Edit: Alright, switched the axes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually wondering why nobody seemed to mention this, as it actually screws up the article. If time was on the x and the instances were on the y,(as most line graphs work) it would depict 2738 instances manifesting at a staggering rate. Instead, the graph depicts 2738 infection slowing down radically. I upvoted, but the graph breaks the immersion quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 but having time on the y-axis is messed up lmao

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love your collapse 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how this is a very mundane, vaguely unimportant sort of thing: anomalous POS's aren't exactly earth-shattering, though the reality-bending aspect certainly has potential to do damage. (I do love the phrase "Economic Anomaly".)

I'm not quite sure I got where this was going, though. The line of questioning about its origin and purpose didn't turn up hardly anything; I'm surprised they didn't try it again somewhere with a more general stock than just used books. I get sort of a "well, what can ya do?" vibe from the people involved, I guess? No real sense of the danger, despite what that graph suggests. No-vote for the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dang that is one sassy virus.

The various test logs are what made this for me. It was interesting to see how the virus responded to individuals inputs, and the responses to Shaw made me chuckle. All around an entertaining premise and a neat skip.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing the praise above. The interviews worked really well.
As a frequenter of bookstores, I liked the WEUB. Imagine working at a used book store that's also a Foundation front; certainly seems less dangerous than a lot of other sites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing scares me more than a computer with access to a credit card.

In all seriousness, I really enjoyed the concept and visuals behind this SCP. It was well-written in my opinion. We don't really have any organized technological anomalies anymore, which is why i'm glad you've written this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really good writing style, with a satisfying story/background/history. I've always had a weak spot for software-related anomalies. The way the true nature of the program is revealed is a refreshing read. New articles like this are relatively rare nowadays.

In light of this, upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's too bad that the end stage of this program is terminal database corruption and a breakdown of commerce, because I get the sense that even though it describes itself as a virus it's still trying to serve the customer at the end of the day.

Yep! It has good intentions for the well-being of people, but has trouble functioning in a capitalist system, and ends up doing harm. You hit the concept on the nose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the tone and flavor of this, particularly the interview log in the bookstore. The brief flashes of intent we get from the program, like when it tries to help the agent who says he's in danger, really add to the personality of the piece.

It's too bad that the end stage of this program is terminal database corruption and a breakdown of commerce, because I get the sense that even though it describes itself as a virus it's still trying to serve the customer at the end of the day. +1

EDIT: I didn't check the axis labels on the graph since in my window size they were illegible without maximizing, but on closer inspection buttfranklin is right. This graph would make a lot more sense with the axis labels reversed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed the progression of how the Foundation figured this out. It's always so much better to have the reader follow the train of thought and come to a conclusion with them rather than just telling them. I also thought the way that the Foundation communicated with it was clever and the last interview was both well-done and builds the universe through a former Foundation employee. Well done!

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded:
Unless otherwise stated, any graphics or images in my articles are made and/or taken by me.

The first draft of this was from July 2016; I got the idea when I was working retail and thought "hey, how come we with boring jobs never get any weird events when all the physicists are discovering wormholes and aliens and whatnot?" I then had a revision that I didn't like, gave up on it again for a month, and then finally reinvigorated it a third time and tied it all together a few days ago. My thanks to
WrongJohnSilver, Roget, Palmetto28, and Maddy L for crit and feedback.

Edit: Tags. Given that I'm junior staff, I should probably stop being scared of tagging my own articles. Added the fitting tags in reference to the tag list, and will let tag team decide if 'uncontained' also applies. (Probably not, since the Foundation is at least aware of it, and is researching full containment.)
